brief: Fling satellites around a planet with real Newtonian gravity and watch Kepler's laws happen live.
prompt: Build a complete orbital mechanics teaching sandbox as a single self-contained HTML file (all CSS and JS inline, no external resources, runs fully offline). Fix a glowing planet at canvas center with gravitational parameter mu and radius R; drag from any empty point (mouse or touch) to launch a satellite where the drag vector sets initial velocity, and while dragging draw a live ghost preview of the resulting conic orbit computed analytically from the vis-viva/orbital elements so the user sees the ellipse or hyperbola before releasing. Integrate up to 8 simultaneous satellites with semi-implicit Euler using delta-time so motion is framerate-independent, each leaving a fading trail capped at 300 points; bound orbits render as thin white ellipses, escape trajectories as dashed red. Tapping a satellite selects it and the HUD panel (top-left) shows its live altitude, speed, apoapsis, periapsis, eccentricity, and period, plus current time-warp and satellite count; on the selected satellite shade the swept radius-vector sector over the last 2 seconds so Kepler's second law (faster at periapsis) is visibly demonstrated. Provide 4 preset buttons — Circular LEO, Eccentric Ellipse, Molniya-style, Escape — each launching a demo satellite with an embedded one-line caption explaining what to notice. Controls: scroll/pinch to zoom the view, Space or an on-screen button pauses, keys 1-4 or on-screen buttons set time warp 1x/10x/100x/1000x, R or a Reset button clears all satellites. Detect crashes when a satellite dips below R: spawn a 20-particle burst and a synthesized WebAudio low thump; play a short blip on every launch, with a mute toggle. Style: dark navy space, soft teal atmosphere ring around the planet, faint starfield of 80 static dots.

title: Journey to the Core
brief: Drag a drill probe 6,371 km down through Earth's layers while temperature and pressure gauges climb.
prompt: Build a complete interactive Earth-interior descent explorer as a single self-contained HTML file (all CSS and JS inline, no external resources, runs fully offline). Render a scrolling vertical cross-section from the surface (0 km) to Earth's center (6371 km) as canvas strata with distinct gradients and subtle procedural texture: crust (0-35 km), upper mantle (35-660 km), lower mantle (660-2890 km), liquid outer core (2890-5150 km, animated slow swirl), solid inner core (5150-6371 km, white-hot glow). A drill probe stays vertically centered while the strata scroll past it; control depth by dragging anywhere with mouse or touch, by mouse wheel, or by holding Up/Down arrows which accelerate, all eased with delta-time critically-damped smoothing so descent speed is framerate-independent. Embed an inline dataset of exactly 24 depth milestones (deepest mine ~4 km, Kola Superdeep Borehole 12.26 km, Challenger Deep equivalent 10.9 km, Moho, 410 km and 660 km transition zones, D'' layer, inner-core boundary, center, etc.), each with name, depth, and a one-sentence fact; when the probe crosses a milestone, slide in its fact card and drop a marker on the depth track. HUD: fixed right-side panel with live depth in km, temperature interpolated from an embedded 12-point geotherm table (surface ~15 C to ~5400 C at center), pressure interpolated to ~360 GPa, and current layer name with solid/liquid state badge; on the left show a whole-Earth minimap disc with the probe's position marked, tappable/clickable to jump to any depth. Play a synthesized WebAudio low rumble (filtered noise) whose gain and pitch rise with depth plus a metallic tick at each milestone, behind a mute toggle. Palette runs cool browns at the surface to blinding orange-white at the core with a heat-shimmer wobble below 5000 km. Include a Reset button that eases the probe back to the surface.

title: Ink Bloom
brief: Drop ink into still water and stir it into curling filaments with your finger.
prompt: Build a complete interactive ink-in-water simulation as a single self-contained HTML file (all CSS and JS inline, no external resources, runs fully offline). Simulate a shallow dish of water on a full-window canvas using a coarse stable-fluids-style solver on a fixed 128x128 velocity+dye grid (semi-Lagrangian advection plus 2-4 Jacobi diffusion iterations and mild velocity damping is sufficient — no full pressure projection required), rendered upscaled with image smoothing so blooms look soft and organic. Controls: click/tap injects an ink drop at the pointer as a soft radial splat with a small outward velocity impulse; press-and-drag stirs the water by injecting velocity along the drag direction so existing filaments curl and follow the stroke; keys 1-4 AND four on-screen color swatch buttons (touch-friendly, 44px minimum) select the ink color from a fixed palette of indigo, vermilion, gold, and sumi black; Space or an on-screen pause button freezes the simulation. Style: rice-paper off-white background with a faint vignette; render dye with density-mapped alpha so thin wisps stay translucent while dense cores go nearly opaque, and blend overlapping inks multiplicatively so colors mix like real pigment. HUD: a top-left readout showing current color, total drops placed, and live FPS, plus a small 'settling' meter that fills as total kinetic energy decays toward stillness. WebAudio (synthesized only, no files): a low-passed noise 'bloop' whose pitch scales inversely with drop size on each injection, and a faint continuous stirring wash whose gain tracks drag speed and fades when the pointer lifts. Step the simulation with delta-time clamped to a 33 ms maximum so motion is framerate-independent, and include a clearly visible Reset button that instantly clears both dye and velocity fields back to clean water.

title: Rose Window
brief: A stained-glass rose window grows pane by pane — backlight it with your cursor, crack it with a click.
prompt: Build a complete interactive stained-glass growth animation as a single self-contained HTML file (all CSS and JS inline, no external resources, runs fully offline). A gothic rose window assembles itself pane by pane at the center of a dark canvas: generate 80-120 glass cells geometrically (a center medallion plus 4 concentric rings subdivided radially with jittered angular borders — pure math, no heavy Voronoi library), then animate panes completing one at a time (~4 per second), each sweeping in with a brightness bloom before settling into a jewel tone drawn from an 8-color palette (ruby, cobalt, amber, emerald, violet, rose, teal, milk white) separated by dark lead lines 3-4 px wide, with slight per-pane brightness noise so the glass feels hand-blown. Controls: mouse-move (or touch-drag) sweeps a soft sunbeam across the window so panes near the pointer glow as if backlit, with smooth distance falloff; click/tap a completed pane to crack it — draw 3-5 jagged fracture polylines radiating from the exact click point clipped inside that pane, dim it, then regrow it over ~2 seconds into a NEW random palette color; Shift+click or long-press (600 ms) on touch shatters an entire ring at once; G toggles a slow ambient glimmer that drifts across all panes. Style: cathedral-dark surround with 2-3 subtle animated volumetric light rays behind the window. HUD: a top bar showing panes built / total, a build progress bar, and a running crack count. WebAudio (synthesized only): a short glass 'ting' (fast-decay sine, pitch varied per pane radius) as each pane completes, and a bright band-passed noise burst on every fracture. Drive all growth, glow, and regrow animation with delta-time so it is framerate-independent, and include a visible Rebuild button that clears the window and regrows it with a fresh random layout and color assignment.

title: Brickwright
brief: Found towers with a click and watch a dusk skyline mason itself into being, brick by brick.
prompt: Build a complete generative-architecture animation as a single self-contained HTML file (all CSS and JS inline, no external resources, runs fully offline). A procedural skyline assembles itself brick by brick on a full-window 2.5D side-view canvas: individual bricks (roughly 10x6 px, scaled to viewport) arc in from above with eased flight, snap onto the current course with a tiny dust puff, and accumulate into buildings generated from a small grammar — width 6-14 bricks, height 10-40 courses, a door, 2-6 window gaps that glow warm yellow once the building tops out, and a roof chosen from flat, gable, or crenellated, with an occasional spired tower — capped at 8 buildings and about 2,500 standing bricks total. Controls: click/tap empty ground to found a new building at that x position (enforce minimum spacing, reject with a shake if too close); click/tap a finished building to demolish it — its bricks tumble with gravity and horizontal scatter (cap active tumbling debris at 400 particles, fading out on rest); mouse-move steers a small crane cursor that marks where the next bricks route; Up/Down arrows AND on-screen +/- buttons adjust build speed between 4 and 120 bricks per second; N regenerates the entire skyline from a new seed. Style: warm terracotta-and-sand brick palette against a dusk gradient sky with a slowly sinking sun disc whose position stretches soft building shadows longer as it drops. HUD: bricks placed, buildings standing, current bricks-per-second rate, and the active random seed displayed top-left. WebAudio (synthesized only): a soft 'tock' per placed brick that degrades gracefully into a granular tick texture when the rate exceeds 20/s, plus a low filtered-noise rumble during demolition. Use delta-time for brick flight, tumble physics, and the sun's descent so everything is framerate-independent, and include a visible Reset button that clears the ground and begins a fresh skyline.

title: Gravity Harp
brief: Fling satellites into orbit and let gravity pluck a 12-string pentatonic harp.
prompt: Build a complete gravity harp instrument as a single self-contained HTML file (all CSS and JS inline, no external resources, runs fully offline). Render a full-viewport canvas with a glowing central attractor and 12 radial strings arranged like spokes, tuned to a hardcoded two-octave A minor pentatonic scale (A2 to A4, frequencies embedded inline). Controls: press and drag with mouse or touch to aim (draw a dotted trajectory line while dragging), release to launch an orbiter whose initial velocity matches drag direction and length; keep at most 8 orbiters, evicting the oldest, and remove any that escape beyond 1.5x the viewport radius. Integrate inverse-square gravity toward the center with delta-time so orbits are framerate-independent. Each time an orbiter crosses a string, pluck it with synthesized WebAudio: a 30ms noise burst through a resonant bandpass at the string frequency layered with a detuned triangle oscillator decaying exponentially over 1.5s, stereo-panned by string angle; animate the plucked string with a decaying sine wobble and a radial light pulse, and give orbiters speed-colored fading trails. HUD shows orbiter count, total plucks, and current root note, with on-screen buttons to cycle the root (A/C/D/E/G retunes all strings), toggle 0.5x slow motion, and Clear All as the reset affordance. Style: deep-indigo space, thin luminous strings, additive glow. Initialize the AudioContext on first user gesture, and make touch behave identically to mouse.

title: Drone Mandala
brief: Raise eight harmonic rings into a breathing ritual drone that chants slow vowels.
prompt: Build a complete overtone drone ritual instrument as a single self-contained HTML file (all CSS and JS inline, no external resources, runs fully offline). Center a mandala of 8 concentric rings on a full-viewport near-black canvas; each ring is one harmonic partial (1x through 8x) of a fundamental drone. Controls: click or touch a ring and drag radially to set that partial's gain from 0 to 1 (show the level as ring thickness and brightness); tap a ring quickly to toggle it between 0 and its last level; all gestures work identically with mouse and touch. Synthesize each partial with WebAudio as a sine oscillator at n times the fundamental through its own gain node, with slow random pitch drift of plus-or-minus 4 cents and a global breathing LFO (0.1 Hz) gently modulating master gain; choose the fundamental from 5 on-screen stone buttons hardcoded to 55.00, 65.41, 73.42, 82.41, and 98.00 Hz. Add a Chant toggle that fades in a quiet sawtooth sub-layer routed through two sweeping bandpass filters whose center frequencies crossfade between embedded formant pairs for the vowels A, O, and U on a 6-second cycle. Visuals: each active ring rotates at a speed proportional to its gain (advance rotation with delta-time), emits drifting spark particles capped at 120, and pulses with the breathing LFO. HUD displays fundamental frequency, count of active partials, a master volume slider, and an Extinguish button that fades all gains to silence over 2 seconds and clears particles as the reset affordance. Style: candle-lit ritual palette of ember orange and bone gold on charcoal, soft additive glow. Start audio only after the first user gesture.

title: Nebula Grains
brief: Smear a cloud of shimmering audio grains across a frozen chord and sculpt it live.
prompt: Build a complete granular texture pad as a single self-contained HTML file (all CSS and JS inline, no external resources, runs fully offline). On load, synthesize a 3-second source AudioBuffer entirely in code: sum five detuned sine partials rooted at 110 Hz with slow amplitude crossfades plus a band-filtered noise swell, normalized inline — no samples or fetches. Render a full-viewport nebula canvas where the horizontal axis maps to playback position within the buffer and the vertical axis maps to pitch via playbackRate from 0.5 to 2.0 (label faint gridlines at octave positions). Controls: press and hold with mouse or touch (support 2 simultaneous touch streams as independent voices) to spawn grains at the pointer at the current density; each grain is a short buffer-source slice of 40 to 200ms with a Hann-shaped gain envelope, position jitter of plus-or-minus 40ms, random stereo pan spread, and pitch taken from pointer Y; faster pointer movement widens the spray radius. Every grain draws a glowing mote that drifts outward and fades over 1 second with hue mapped to pitch, capped at 150 live motes, animated with delta-time. HUD panel provides sliders for grain size (40-200ms), density (5-50 grains per second), and jitter, a wet knob for a feedback delay effect (0.3s delay, 0.35 feedback), a Freeze toggle that locks playback position for a sustained drone at the last touch point, and a Reset button that kills all grains, clears motes, and restores default slider values. Style: deep violet-teal nebula with additive bloom and a subtle starfield backdrop. Resume the AudioContext on first gesture and show a live readout of grains per second and active voices in the HUD.

title: Village Quest
brief: Tiny Zelda-like: chat up the villagers, grab the key, clear one slime-guarded dungeon room, loot the chest, come home a hero.
prompt: Build a complete top-down retro adventure game as a single self-contained HTML file (all CSS and JS inline, no external resources, runs fully offline). Render two hand-authored 24x16-tile maps on a canvas (16px tiles, integer-scaled with image-rendering:pixelated, NES-era palette of ~12 colors, every sprite drawn in code with fillRect — no images), each map encoded as an inline array of exactly 16 row-strings of 24 characters (e.g. 'W' water, 'T' tree, 'H' house wall, 'D' dungeon door): a village overworld with grass, dirt paths, a water border, 3 houses with solid walls, tree clusters, and a locked dungeon entrance to the north, plus ONE dungeon room containing 2 slimes and a treasure chest — no puzzles, no boss. Controls: Arrow keys or WASD move the hero (~4 tiles/sec with smooth delta-time motion and solid-tile collision), Space/Enter or the A button interacts and swings the sword; always render an on-screen d-pad (bottom-left) and A button (bottom-right) that work via touch and mouse. Place 2 NPCs who each show one line of dialogue in a bottom text box when interacted — the shopkeeper must hint 'The rusty key behind my house opens the dungeon up north... beware the slimes' — and put the key pickup directly behind that house so the full loop (grab key, unlock door, kill or dodge the 2 slimes patrolling fixed back-and-forth paths, open the chest) is completable in under 30 seconds. Combat: the sword swing spawns a brief 1-tile arc hitbox, slimes die in 2 hits with knockback on hit; touching a slime costs half a heart and grants 1s of invulnerability flashing. HUD: 3 heart containers, a key icon when held, and the current area name; at 0 hearts show a GAME OVER overlay with a Restart button, and opening the chest warps the hero back to the village and shows a QUEST COMPLETE banner — also keep a small Reset button visible at all times. Synthesize exactly 3 chiptune-style WebAudio stingers (square and triangle oscillators, fast envelopes): sword swing, key/chest pickup, and the victory jingle. Drive everything with requestAnimationFrame using delta-time so movement and slime patrols are framerate-independent, and keep both maps as compact inline row-string literals.

title: Echo Vanguard
brief: Every 12-second loop your past runs replay as ghost allies fighting beside you.
prompt: Build a complete time-echo arena shooter as a single self-contained HTML file (all CSS and JS inline, no external resources, runs fully offline). Play unfolds in fixed 12-second loops on a 640x640 canvas arena: during each loop the player pilots a small vector ship, and when the loop ends the arena resets, enemies respawn stronger, and a translucent ghost 'echo' of the player's previous run replays its exact recorded positions and shots as an allied ship — keep a rolling maximum of 4 echoes (oldest is dropped) by recording position and fire events every frame into per-loop arrays. Controls: the ship steers smoothly toward the mouse or touch-drag point with acceleration and drag, holding the mouse button or touch fires forward-facing bolts at 6 shots/s; WASD/arrow keys also move and Space fires as a keyboard alternative. Enemies spawn from the four edges — exactly 2 types: drifters that home slowly toward the player, and splitters that break into 2 small fast shards when destroyed — with the on-screen enemy count capped at 14, starting at 4 spawns in loop 1 and adding 2 per loop. Track and display in a HUD: current loop number, a prominent countdown bar for the 12s loop timer, total kills, number of active echoes, and 3 hull points; enemy contact costs 1 hull with 1s invulnerability flicker, and hull 0 ends the run. Visual style: retro CRT vector phosphor — glowing green player, amber enemies, ghostly blue-white echoes at 40% opacity with afterglow motion trails and a faint curved-screen vignette. Synthesize WebAudio only: a short square-wave laser blip per shot, a shimmering upward sweep when a new echo materializes at loop start, and filtered-noise explosions. Use delta-time so motion and the recorded echo playback are framerate-independent, show a RUN OVER panel with loops survived and total kills, and provide an always-visible Restart button plus R key that clears all echoes and starts fresh.

title: Umbra Heist
brief: Slip between sweeping searchlight cones and steal the vault's data cores without being seen.
prompt: Build a complete light-and-shadow stealth arcade game as a single self-contained HTML file (all CSS and JS inline, no external resources, runs fully offline). Top-down view on a 720x720 canvas vault floor: guide a small thief avatar using WASD/arrow keys, or by holding the mouse button or a touch anywhere to steer the thief toward the pointer with smooth acceleration and friction. Exactly 4 corner watchtowers each sweep a rotating 55-degree translucent searchlight cone (rotation speeds between 0.4 and 0.9 rad/s, alternating directions), and exactly 5 fixed rectangular pillars both block movement and block light — the player counts as lit only when inside a cone's angular sector AND the straight segment from that tower to the player intersects none of the 5 pillar rectangles, so hiding behind pillars visibly works; give the thief a glowing outline when lit and a dim outline when safely in shadow. While lit, a detection meter fills over 1.2 seconds (draining twice as fast in shadow); a full meter triggers a red alarm flash, costs 1 of 3 lives, and respawns the thief at the entry pad. Objective: collect 8 glowing data cores scattered across the floor, each pickup raising every cone's sweep speed by 10%, then reach the pulsing exit pad to win; show a victory panel with elapsed time. Track and display in a HUD: cores collected out of 8, the detection meter as a bar, lives remaining, and a run timer. Visual style: retro-futurist heist noir — near-black indigo floor with a faint grid, harsh amber gradient cones with soft edges, cyan chromatic-glow cores, stark geometric pillars. Synthesize WebAudio only: a rising two-tone alert that plays while the player is lit, a soft chime per core collected, and a deep klaxon on capture. Use delta-time so sweeps and movement are framerate-independent, and include an always-visible Restart button plus the R key to reset the heist instantly.

title: Wobble Jelly
brief: Grab, fling, and squash a pressurized jelly blob that always wobbles back.
prompt: Build a complete squishy jelly-blob fidget toy as a single self-contained HTML file (all CSS and JS inline, no external resources, runs fully offline). Simulate one pressurized soft body: 24 perimeter points connected by springs to their neighbors and to the blob centroid, plus an internal pressure force pushing outward in proportion to how far the current enclosed area falls below a target rest area, so the blob always re-inflates after a squish. Integrate with semi-implicit Euler using clamped delta-time so motion is framerate-independent, apply gravity onto the floor of a room filling the viewport, restitution around 0.4 against all four walls, and about 4 constraint relaxation iterations per frame for stability. Controls: mouse-drag or touch-drag grabs the nearest perimeter point (or the whole blob when grabbed near the centroid) with a stiff spring to the pointer; releasing while moving flings it with the pointer velocity; double-click or double-tap delivers a radial poke impulse that dents the surface. Render a glossy gradient body with a specular highlight, a soft ellipse shadow that widens as the blob squashes, and two googly eyes that lag and deform with the surface for charm. Audio: a WebAudio pitch-bent boing on release whose start frequency scales with current deformation, and a low thud on wall impacts scaled by impact speed. HUD: live squish percentage (current vs rest area) and best fling-speed record. Include a Reset button that re-centers the blob at rest.

title: Clink Curtain
brief: Swipe through hanging bead chains that clink, tangle, and coil on the floor.
prompt: Build a complete hanging bead-chain curtain fidget toy as a single self-contained HTML file (all CSS and JS inline, no external resources, runs fully offline). Simulate 5 chains of 22 beads each (radius about 7px) hanging from anchor points spaced along a top rail, using Verlet integration with gravity, slight air damping, distance constraints solved in 6 iterations per frame, and clamped delta-time so motion is framerate-independent. Beads collide with beads of neighboring chains via simple sphere push-apart and with the floor, so chains shove each other aside and detached chains coil into piles instead of passing through anything. Controls: mouse-drag or touch-drag grabs the nearest bead and pins it to the pointer while the rest of the chain trails naturally; releasing while moving flings the chain; fast swipes through the curtain scatter all chains; clicking or tapping a chain's rail anchor detaches that chain so it drops and piles on the floor, and clicking the empty anchor re-hangs it. Audio: synthesize short triangle-wave clinks with WebAudio (randomized 1800-2600 Hz, fast decay) whenever a bead collision exceeds a velocity threshold, throttled to at most 12 clinks per second, volume scaled by impact speed. Render metallic beads with radial-gradient specular highlights on a deep charcoal background, the rail as a brushed metal bar. HUD: a total kinetic-energy meter and a count of hanging vs dropped chains. Include a Reset button that re-hangs and stills every chain.

title: Voltage Keys
brief: A pocket hardware synth: three neon voices, a glowing oscilloscope, and it lights up if you plug in a real MIDI keyboard.
prompt: Build a complete playable synthesizer keyboard with live audio visualization as a single self-contained HTML file (all CSS and JS inline, no external resources, runs fully offline). Render a 25-key on-screen piano spanning C3-C5 with correctly offset black keys, playable by mouse and multi-touch via pointer events (sliding a finger across keys retriggers notes, releasing anywhere stops them) and by computer keys A/W/S/E/D/F/T/G/Y/H/U/J/K mapped to one octave with Z/X shifting the playable range down/up one octave. Implement 3 selectable synth voices as WebAudio oscillator recipes chosen by labeled buttons: FAT SAW (3 sawtooth oscillators detuned -12/0/+12 cents through a 2.5 kHz lowpass), FM BELL (sine carrier plus sine modulator at a 3.5x frequency ratio with modulation depth ~200 Hz decaying quickly for a metallic strike), and SQUARE LEAD (square wave with a short pitch-blip attack); give every note a per-note ADSR gain envelope (attack ~0.02s, decay ~0.15s, sustain 0.6, release ~0.35s) with two on-screen sliders adjusting attack and release live, support at least 8-voice polyphony with proper noteOn/noteOff bookkeeping so no notes stick, and route everything through a master volume slider into an AnalyserNode. Above the keys draw a canvas visualization updated with requestAnimationFrame and delta-time so decay speeds are framerate-independent: a glowing oscilloscope trace from getByteTimeDomainData overlaid on 48 mirrored frequency bars from getByteFrequencyData that flare per note and fall smoothly to silence. Show a HUD displaying current voice, octave range, live polyphony count, and the note names currently held; pressed keys must visibly depress and tint with the active voice's accent color. Add Web MIDI strictly as progressive enhancement: only if navigator.requestMIDIAccess is available, request access, wire noteon/noteoff from all inputs with velocity scaling the note gain, and show a green 'MIDI CONNECTED: <device name>' badge (otherwise show 'NO MIDI - touch/keys active'); the entire app must be fully playable without MIDI. Style it as dark hardware-synth chic: brushed-charcoal panel, one neon accent per voice (orange/teal/magenta), rounded keycaps with soft shadows. Unlock the AudioContext on the first user gesture and include a PANIC/reset button that kills all sounding voices and clears any stuck notes.

title: Energy Web
brief: Watch glowing energy pips climb a three-level food chain, then play god with grass, grazers, and predators.
prompt: Build a complete predator-prey ecosystem simulation as a single self-contained HTML file (all CSS and JS inline, no external resources, runs fully offline). Render a 900x540 canvas meadow with three trophic levels: up to 60 grass tufts that regrow on individual timers, up to 30 grazers (soft green dots) that wander with smooth noise steering, seek the nearest grass within 120px, and eat it, and up to 8 predators (red triangles) that chase the nearest grazer within 180px. Give every creature a visible energy bar above it; each eat transfers energy as 3-5 glowing amber pips that visibly fly along an arc from prey to eater, reproduction triggers above an energy threshold (the creature splits with a small pop ring, each half keeping half the energy), and starvation fades a creature to grey before it dies and leaves a fertile patch where grass regrows twice as fast. Controls: click or tap to plant a cluster of 5 grass tufts at the cursor, drag to scatter grass along the path, and press G/H/P (mirrored by three labeled on-screen touch buttons) to spawn one grass tuft, grazer, or predator at a random spot. HUD: live counts for all three populations plus a scrolling 60-second stacked sparkline of population history, and a flashing COLLAPSE banner naming any level that hits zero. Synthesize WebAudio feedback: a soft tick on each eat, a rising two-note chirp on birth, a low thud on death. Use delta-time so wandering, chasing, and pip flight are framerate-independent, enforce the population caps above, and include a Reset button that reseeds a balanced world of 40 grass, 18 grazers, and 4 predators. Style: dark soil background, bioluminescent glowing creatures, faint motion-trail fade on the canvas.

title: Rainmaker
brief: Seed clouds, steer the wind, and terraform a procedural desert into forest one rainstorm at a time.
prompt: Build a complete weather-seeding terraforming game as a single self-contained HTML file (all CSS and JS inline, no external resources, runs fully offline). Generate a 56x32 grid of 16px tiles (896x512 canvas) using smooth value-noise elevation, with tiles below a water threshold rendered as 4-6 small lakes and everything else starting as dry sand shaded by height. Controls: click or tap to seed a cloud at the cursor (spending one seed from a budget that starts at 6 and regenerates one every 5 seconds up to 10, max 8 clouds alive at once), drag anywhere to set the global wind vector shown as a compass arrow, and use arrow keys to nudge wind as a keyboard alternative. Each cloud is a drifting cluster of 6-10 soft puff circles with a shadow offset on the ground below, carries water from 0-100, absorbs 8 water per second while over lake tiles, and rains automatically onto the 3x3 tiles beneath when saturation exceeds 70 or when pushed over elevation above 0.7 (orographic rain), then dissipates when empty. Rain raises tile moisture; a tile that stays above moisture 40 advances one vegetation stage every 4 seconds through sand, scrub, grass, and forest, while moisture evaporates continuously and tiles below 10 regress one stage every 6 seconds so deserts creep back if you stop tending. HUD: percent of land tiles at grass-or-better, best percent this session, seed count, and the wind vane. Synthesize WebAudio: a looping filtered white-noise rain bed whose gain tracks the number of currently raining clouds, plus a low sine thunder thump when a cloud dissipates. Use delta-time for cloud drift, absorption, and growth timers, and include a Restart button that re-rolls the terrain seed and resets the budget. Style: warm ochre-to-lush-green palette, soft tile color lerps between stages, gentle cloud bobbing.

title: Dot Empires
brief: Four tribes of dots forage, trade, and wage war over shifting territory while you tip the balance with bounties.
prompt: Build a complete micro-civilization ecosystem simulation as a single self-contained HTML file (all CSS and JS inline, no external resources, runs fully offline). On a 900x560 canvas, spawn 4 color-coded tribes (teal, rust, gold, violet), each starting with one town square and 12 citizen dots, plus 14 neutral resource nodes scattered with minimum spacing; dots pick the nearest unclaimed-rich node, forage, and visibly haul a carried speck back to their town, which banks wealth and spawns a new dot every 8 banked units up to 40 dots per tribe, and founds a second town at 120 wealth. Maintain a pairwise relations score from -3 to +3 for all tribe pairs: when dots of two tribes meet at the same node they trade if relations are 0 or higher (gold sparkle, both tribes gain 2 wealth, relations +0.5) and fight if negative (red flash, the loser dot dies, relations -0.5), and trigger an envy event that drops relations by 1 whenever one tribe's wealth exceeds double another's; a town falls and its dots scatter when 5+ enemy dots raid it. Shade the whole map at low alpha by nearest-town color so territory borders visibly shift as towns rise and fall. Controls: click or tap to drop a 20-unit bounty node that attracts every tribe's dots and forces contact, long-press or right-click a town to gift it 10 wealth, and press keys 1-4 or tap a tribe's HUD row to toggle an outline highlight following that tribe. HUD: per-tribe population and wealth bars, a 4x4 relations grid tinted green-to-red, and a 5-line scrolling event ticker with lines like 'Teal and Gold struck a trade pact' and 'War: Rust sacked a Violet town'. Synthesize WebAudio: a pentatonic chime on trade, a short drum hit on each fight, a deep gong when a town falls. Use delta-time for all movement and timers, add a 1x/4x speed toggle button, and include a Reset button that re-rolls town and node placement. Style: dark parchment-map background, glowing dots, thin roads fading in along frequently traveled paths.

title: Pixel Colony
brief: Paint food, drop creatures, raise walls, trigger droughts — then watch color-coded lineages evolve, bloom, and crash in real time.
prompt: Build a complete pixel colony life simulator as a single self-contained HTML file (all CSS and JS inline, no external resources, runs fully offline). Render a 96x64 cell world on a canvas scaled to fill the viewport with crisp nearest-neighbor pixels on a dark earthy background: green food cells that regrow by spreading into adjacent empty soil with ~2% chance per tick, and 1-cell creatures that each carry energy, hue, age, and generation. Creatures wander randomly with a bias toward the nearest food within a 6-cell radius, eat food they step onto (+8 energy), burn 1 energy per tick, reproduce when energy exceeds 20 by splitting into an adjacent free cell — the child inherits the parent's hue shifted by a random ±10 degree mutation so lineages visibly drift into distinct color families over minutes — and die at zero energy or ~1200 ticks of age, leaving a corpse pixel that fades over 30 ticks. Seed the world with 40 creatures in 3 clustered hue families and ~15% food coverage; hard-cap population at 600. The player intervenes through a tool palette of five buttons (Food, Creature, Wall, Drought, Erase) with drag-painting on the canvas via mouse and touch: Food paints regrowable food in a 3-cell brush, Creature drops one random-hue creature per tap or drag step, Wall paints impassable gray cells creatures path around, Drought kills all food in a 5-cell brush, Erase clears walls and food; highlight the active tool button. Show a HUD with live population, total births, total deaths, tick count, and a dominant-hue swatch, plus three scrolling strip charts along the bottom edge (last 300 samples each, labeled): population, average age, and max generation reached. Provide speed controls: Pause, a single Step button that advances exactly one tick while paused, and 1x/4x/16x speed buttons; drive the simulation with a fixed-tick accumulator fed by requestAnimationFrame delta-time so simulation speed is framerate-independent. Add subtle synthesized WebAudio feedback (a soft rate-limited blip on births, a low thump when a drought stroke lands) with a mute toggle, and a Reset button that reseeds the entire world.

title: Atlas of Ideas
brief: A living constellation of 45 encyclopedia topics — click a node and knowledge blooms around it.
prompt: Build a complete knowledge topic graph explorer as a single self-contained HTML file (all CSS and JS inline, no external resources, runs fully offline). Embed a curated dataset you write yourself: exactly 45 topic nodes spanning 5 categories (science, history, art, technology, music — 9 per category), each with a title, category, and a plausible one-sentence encyclopedia-style summary, plus ~90 weighted links (weight 1-3) connecting sensibly related topics across categories (e.g. Photosynthesis—Oxygen, Renaissance—Perspective Drawing) — 135 entries total; summaries need only read plausibly, not be rigorously factual, but link pairings should feel sensible. Render on a full-viewport canvas with a force-directed layout: spring attraction along links scaled by weight, pairwise repulsion, gentle centering gravity, and velocity damping so the graph visibly settles within ~4 seconds; integrate with delta-time so motion is framerate-independent. Draw links with thickness and opacity proportional to weight, nodes as category-colored discs sized by degree, and labels that appear above a zoom threshold and always on the focused node and its neighbors. Controls: click/tap a node to focus it — smoothly pan/zoom it to center, highlight its neighbors while dimming everything else to ~15% opacity, and open a right-side panel (bottom sheet under 700px width) showing its title, category chip, summary, and a clickable list of linked neighbors that refocuses on tap; drag nodes with mouse or touch (pinned while held, released back into physics); drag empty space to pan; mouse wheel and two-finger pinch to zoom (0.3x-3x); Escape or clicking empty space clears focus. Show a HUD with node count, link count, current zoom percent, selected topic name, and a settling/settled physics indicator, plus a static color-coded category legend. Style it as a dark space chart — near-black background, softly glowing nodes, thin luminous links, clean sans-serif labels. Provide a Reset button that clears focus, resets zoom to fit, and reheats the simulation for a fresh settle.

title: Numbers From Nowhere
brief: Tune a haunted shortwave band where five ghost stations hide in the static.
prompt: Build a complete shortwave void-receiver as a single self-contained HTML file (all CSS and JS inline, no external resources, runs fully offline). Render a dark radio faceplate: brushed-black panel, amber dial lamp, and a canvas tuning band from 4000 to 8000 kHz with a needle. Controls: drag the needle with mouse or touch to tune, Left/Right arrows fine-tune 1 kHz, Shift+arrows 10 kHz. Synthesize all audio with WebAudio (unlocked on first pointer interaction): a continuous band-limited noise bed, plus exactly 5 hidden stations at seeded pseudo-random frequencies — a looping Morse beacon spelling a 5-character callsign, a numbers station reading 4-digit groups as dual-tone beeps with a chime between groups, a detuned 3-oscillator drone choir, a slow heartbeat pulse, and a rising swept howler. Each station has a capture width of ±12 kHz with signal-to-noise crossfade proportional to proximity so voices resolve out of static as the needle approaches center. Draw a live scrolling spectrogram waterfall strip (canvas, about 120 columns) where stations appear as bright traces the user can hunt visually. HUD: frequency readout, 0–100 signal-strength meter, and a logbook that auto-records a station after holding within ±3 kHz for 2 seconds, listing frequency and a cryptic assigned name; logging all 5 combines their callsign fragments into a revealed coordinates string and turns the dial lamp green. Use delta-time for needle inertia (slight analog lag and overshoot) and waterfall scroll. Include a master volume slider and a RESEED button that randomizes the 5 frequencies and clears the logbook. If navigator.requestMIDIAccess is available, map a MIDI CC knob to the tuning dial as progressive enhancement only — the on-screen drag controls must be fully functional without it.

title: The Resonant Reliquary
brief: Rotate four engraved bronze rings by ear until the hum locks pure and the lid irises open.
prompt: Build a complete occult puzzle-box game as a single self-contained HTML file (all CSS and JS inline, no external resources, runs fully offline). Draw on canvas a bronze reliquary lid with 4 concentric engraved rings (radii roughly 70, 110, 150, 190 px), each carved with 12 procedurally stroked rune glyphs (no fonts or images), over a dark stone vignette with candle-like flicker lighting. Each ring has a secret target angle randomized in 30-degree steps at start. Controls: click or touch a ring to select it (highlight rim), drag around the center to rotate it with momentum and damping, or press Left/Right to step the selected ring 6 degrees and Tab to cycle selection — full touch parity. Core discoverable mechanic: each ring emits a synthesized WebAudio sine hum against a low reference drone; far from target it beats harshly and detuned, purifying as the ring nears its angle, and within 6 degrees it locks with a soft chime, its glyphs ignite in gold, and it seals immovably. Twist: locking a ring perturbs one random unlocked neighbor by 6–12 degrees, forcing the player to find a locking order. HUD: four small resonance-meter arcs (one per ring), locked count 0/4, elapsed time, and move counter. On all 4 locks play an opening sequence — rings sink, the lid irises open with light shafts and 40–60 drifting dust-mote particles, revealing a procedurally drawn floating relic eye and the verdict 'The reliquary accepts you' with final time and moves. Use delta-time for ring momentum, flicker, and particles; start audio on first interaction. Include a NEW RELIQUARY button that reseeds targets and resets the timer.

title: The Sigil Scribe
brief: Type an intent and a spectral quill distills it into a glowing sigil you charge and burn.
prompt: Build a complete spectral sigil-scribing machine as a single self-contained HTML file (all CSS and JS inline, no external resources, runs fully offline). Show a black parchment canvas under a candlelight vignette with a single input field placeholder 'inscribe your intent'. On Enter, distill the phrase chaos-magick style: strip vowels and duplicate letters, then map each surviving letter deterministically (simple hash) to one of 22 glyph nodes arranged on an invisible circle so the same phrase always produces the same sigil. Animate a glowing quill point traveling node to node at constant speed, drawing the connecting path with slight hand-tremor wobble from a noise offset, leaving luminous ink that dries from cyan-white to deep violet; ornament the start node with a small circle and the end node with a crossbar. WebAudio (unlocked on first interaction): filtered-noise quill scratch keyed to drawing speed plus a faint band-passed whisper bed with a slow LFO while drawing. When complete the sigil idles with a breathing glow; hold mouse or touch on it to CHARGE — glow and a rising harmonic swell build over 2 seconds shown on a charge meter — and release to BURN: the path ignites stroke-by-stroke in reverse, shedding 100–150 ember particles with gravity and flicker, leaving a faint scorch ghost before the canvas clears for the next intent. HUD: the distilled letters rendered as small runes, the charge meter while holding, and a count of sigils burned this session. Use delta-time for quill travel, particle physics, and glow phases. Include 3 example-phrase buttons for an instant demo and a CLEAR reset button; ensure full mouse and touch parity.
