title: Neon Ruins FPS (agent-built)
brief: GOD MODE harness task: raycast/pseudo-3D sci-fi exploration game with matching level manifest.
prompt: Agent-arena artifact group: outputs of the av2-god-10-neon-ruins-fps harness task.

title: Tilt Temple
brief: Tilt the whole stone labyrinth to roll a marble past pit holes to the goal cup.
prompt: Build a complete tilt-the-board marble labyrinth game as a single self-contained HTML file (all CSS and JS inline, no external resources, runs fully offline). Render a top-down stone maze board on a canvas; the player never touches the marble directly — moving the mouse (or dragging on touch) away from the board center tilts the entire board, and arrow keys tilt in fixed increments as a keyboard alternative. The tilt vector becomes the marble's acceleration with rolling friction and slight restitution off walls, integrated with delta-time so physics is framerate-independent. Sell the tilt visually: skew the board shadow, slide a soft specular highlight across the stone, and let a small on-screen bubble level show the current tilt. Include 5 hand-authored levels of increasing difficulty defined as inline data: raised walls, round pit holes that swallow the marble (it visibly sinks with a shrinking spiral and a descending WebAudio boop, then respawns at the level start), 2-3 collectible brass gems per level that must all be gathered before the goal cup opens, and one level featuring a narrow bridge between two pits. The goal cup captures the marble only below a speed threshold. HUD: level number, gems collected, fall count, and a per-level timer; show a level-complete card with time and falls, and a final scorecard after level 5 with a replay option. Synthesize all audio with WebAudio: a low rolling rumble whose gain and pitch track marble speed, a wooden clack on wall hits scaled by impact, a chime per gem, and a triumphant three-note figure on level clear. Style: warm sandstone board, dark pit shadows, glinting marble with a moving highlight. Include an always-visible Reset Level button and full mouse/touch parity.

title: Gravity Golf
brief: Putt a ball through space where planets bend every shot into a curve.
prompt: Build a complete gravity golf game as a single self-contained HTML file (all CSS and JS inline, no external resources, runs fully offline). Each hole is a starfield canvas containing 1-4 planets that pull the ball with inverse-square gravity (softened near the surface), a tee pad, and a glowing wormhole ring as the cup. Aim by pressing on the ball and dragging backward like a slingshot with mouse or touch; while aiming, draw a live ghost trajectory computed by simulating the first 2 seconds of flight through the actual gravity field, updating as the drag changes, plus a power meter capped at a maximum. Release to launch; the ball coasts under gravity with no friction, leaving a fading trail, until it either enters the wormhole slowly enough to be captured (too fast and it visibly slingshots through with a fizzle), falls into a planet (crater puff, stroke penalty, replaced on the tee), or drifts out of bounds beyond 1.5x the viewport (auto-returned to the last rest point). Include 6 hand-authored holes as inline data with escalating ideas: a single attractor, a binary pair, a slalom of small moons, a hole requiring a full orbital loop, a red giant with a wide kill radius, and a finale combining them; give each hole a par. HUD: hole number, strokes, par, and a running total; after hole 6 show a scorecard with per-hole strokes and a replay button. Planets get subtle procedural banding and soft atmosphere glows, and each has a faint dashed ring showing its strong-gravity zone. Synthesize WebAudio: a soft thunk on launch, a rising shimmer while the ball rides close to a planet (gain tracks gravitational pull), a warm capture chord on sinking the ball, and a dull crater thud. Use delta-time integration so trajectories are framerate-independent, and include an always-visible Retry Hole button.

title: Heliotrope
brief: Steer a living vine's growing tip toward drifting sunlight before it starves in the dark.
prompt: Build a complete vine-growing arcade game as a single self-contained HTML file (all CSS and JS inline, no external resources, runs fully offline). A vine grows continuously upward from the bottom of a portrait canvas at constant speed; the player steers only the growth tip's curvature — hold Left/Right arrows (or A/D), or press-and-hold on the left or right half of the screen on touch, to bend the tip smoothly, giving snake-like but organic control. The stem is stored as a polyline; sprout small alternating leaves every few segments and open a pink blossom every 500 points for visible progress. Sunlight comes as 8-14 drifting golden sun-motes that slowly sink and wander; each mote collected by the tip refills a light meter that otherwise drains constantly — at zero the vine wilts (desaturate and droop the last segments) and the run ends. Obstacles generated procedurally as the view scrolls upward with the tip: rock outcrops jutting from the sides and floating thorn clusters; touching either snaps the vine and ends the run with a falling-petals game-over overlay showing height climbed and blossoms opened, plus a restart prompt. Crossing the vine's own stem is allowed and should visibly weave over it. Difficulty ramps with height: motes get sparser, thorns denser, and a slow shade line chases from below. HUD: height in meters, light meter as an arcing gauge around the tip, blossom count, and best height this session. Synthesize WebAudio: a soft harp pluck per mote (pitch rises with combo streak), a low woody crack on death, and a gentle wind bed whose gain follows scroll speed. Use delta-time for growth, drift, and scrolling so play is framerate-independent, style it as dawn-lit garden air with soft gradients and dust motes, and include an always-visible Restart button.

title: Glowherd
brief: Your lantern scares the glow-critters — herd them into the pen by chasing from the right side.
prompt: Build a complete herding arcade game as a single self-contained HTML file (all CSS and JS inline, no external resources, runs fully offline). On a dark meadow canvas, a scattered herd of glowing critters (soft teal blobs with tiny trailing light, wandering with smooth noise steering and mild mutual clumping) must be driven into a glowing pen circle before the round timer expires. The player IS the pressure: the cursor (or touch point) is a lantern that critters flee from with force falling off over about 140px, so the only way to move the herd is to approach from the opposite side — classic sheepdog dynamics. Clicking or tapping emits a visible ring pulse that startles critters in a wider radius with a stronger impulse (limited to one pulse per 1.5 seconds, shown on a cooldown ring around the lantern). Critters inside the pen for 1 continuous second settle, dim to a calm amber, and stop counting as loose; settled critters startled by a careless pulse can escape again. Include 5 rounds of escalating design as inline data: round 1 has 8 critters and a large static pen, later rounds add more critters, jitterier temperaments, fence obstacles that block critters but not the lantern, a slowly drifting pen, and a final round with two herds of different colors that must go to matching pens. HUD: round number, penned/total count, round timer bar, and a running score rewarding time remaining. Failing a round (timer expires) offers a retry; clearing round 5 shows a total-score celebration with looping firefly confetti. Synthesize WebAudio: soft scatter chirps when critters startle, a warm settling tone per penned critter, and a low pulse whomp. Use delta-time steering so motion is framerate-independent, keep mouse and touch behavior identical, and include an always-visible Restart Round button.

title: Sandfall Alchemist
brief: Place scarce grains of sand, water, seed, and fire into a falling-sand world to fill the flask.
prompt: Build a complete falling-sand alchemy puzzle game as a single self-contained HTML file (all CSS and JS inline, no external resources, runs fully offline). Simulate a cellular-automaton world on a grid of about 160x120 cells rendered crisply to a canvas: SAND falls and piles at an angle of repose; WATER falls, spreads sideways, and pools; SEED sprouts into a VINE that grows upward and branches while touching water; FIRE rises, ignites vines, burns them away, and is quenched by water into drifting STEAM that rises and condenses back to water droplets on stone ceilings; STONE is immutable terrain. Each level is a hand-authored stone layout (inline data) containing an open glass flask, and gives the player a strictly limited budget of element grains shown as labeled inventory chips (for example 120 sand, 80 water, 3 seed, 2 fire); paint elements with click-drag or touch-drag using a 3-cell brush, spending budget per cell, with the active element chip highlighted. The goal per level: fill the flask's marked interior with at least a target number of cells of a SPECIFIED substance (water in one level, sand in another, one level requiring steam condensed around a chicane, one requiring burning a vine bridge at the right moment). A live gauge on the flask shows progress and turns gold at target. Include at least 5 levels of escalating trickiness, a Run/Pause toggle plus a Step button, and per-level retry that restores the budget. HUD: level number, remaining budget per element, flask gauge, and a win banner advancing to the next level with a final all-clear card. Synthesize WebAudio: granular hiss while sand flows, a soft gurgle for pooling water, a crackle for fire, and a glass chime at target fill. Use a fixed-tick accumulator driven by requestAnimationFrame delta-time so simulation speed is framerate-independent, and include an always-visible Reset Level button.

title: Cavern Swing
brief: Fire a rope, swing on the pendulum, release into the dark — momentum is the only way across.
prompt: Build a complete grapple-swing traversal game as a single self-contained HTML file (all CSS and JS inline, no external resources, runs fully offline). A side-scrolling cavern on a canvas: the ceiling carries procedurally spaced glowing anchor crystals, the floor bristles with stalagmites over a black pool, and the view scrolls with the player. The avatar is a small spelunker with simple ballistic physics under gravity. Press and hold the mouse button, touch, or Spacebar to fire a rope to the best anchor (nearest anchor within range in the aiming half-plane toward the pointer, or simply the nearest forward-up anchor when using Spacebar); while held, the rope acts as a rigid pendulum constraint so the player swings, conserving tangential momentum, and releasing detaches into free flight — chaining swings builds speed. Render the rope as a slightly sagging curve of a few Verlet segments so it feels physical, with a taut snap when loaded. Falling into the pool or clipping a stalagmite ends the run with a splash or a clatter, a brief slow-motion replay of the final second, and a game-over card showing distance traveled and max speed reached, plus a restart prompt. Difficulty ramps with distance: wider anchor gaps, low ceiling sections demanding shallow swings, and occasional moving anchors that drift on small loops. Score is distance; scatter bonus fireflies that add points when swept through. HUD: distance, speed, best distance this session, and a subtle rope-range indicator around the player while aiming. Synthesize WebAudio: a rope-whip snap on attach, a creak whose pitch tracks swing load, wind rush scaled with speed, and a deep splash on drowning. Use delta-time physics with a clamped timestep so swings are framerate-independent, style it in bioluminescent blues against near-black rock with faint parallax layers, and include an always-visible Restart button.

title: Prism Loom
brief: Rotate mirrors and prisms so one white beam splits and weaves into every colored target.
prompt: Build a complete light-routing puzzle game as a single self-contained HTML file (all CSS and JS inline, no external resources, runs fully offline). On a dark grid board (about 10x8 cells) drawn on canvas, a fixed emitter fires a continuous white beam. The player clicks or taps placed optical pieces to rotate them in 45-degree steps (right-click or long-press rotates the other way): MIRRORS reflect beams, SPLITTERS pass and reflect simultaneously, PRISMS split a white beam into red, green, and blue beams fanning at 45 degrees apart, COLOR FILTERS pass only their color, and CROSS tiles let beams pass through both axes. Retrace the full beam network instantly on every rotation: propagate rays cell by cell, additively blend overlapping colors (red plus green reads as yellow where they cross), cap total bounces to prevent infinite loops, and render every ray as a glowing line with soft bloom and slowly drifting dust motes inside the beams. Each TARGET jewel lights only when it receives exactly its printed color (including composite colors like yellow or magenta formed by overlapping beams in later levels); all targets lit wins the level with a bloom flourish. Include at least 6 hand-authored levels as inline data with escalating ideas: single mirror warm-up, first prism, filters, a composite-color target, a level with two emitters, and a dense finale; pieces on some levels are locked (drawn bolted) while others rotate. HUD: level number, targets lit versus total, rotation move counter, and a per-level minimum-moves par with a star for matching it. Synthesize WebAudio: a soft click per rotation, a rising harmonic tone as each target lights (chord builds as more light), and a resolve chime on completion. Everything is deterministic with no randomness at solve time, works identically with mouse and touch, and includes an always-visible Reset Level button plus Previous/Next level controls.

title: Thermal Drifter
brief: One control — the burner. Read the winds and ride invisible thermals through the checkpoint course.
prompt: Build a complete hot-air balloon piloting game as a single self-contained HTML file (all CSS and JS inline, no external resources, runs fully offline). Side view on a wide scrolling canvas: the balloon's ONLY direct control is the burner — hold the mouse button, touch, or Spacebar to fire it, heating the envelope (visible flame, glow, and a heat gauge); heat bleeds away naturally, and buoyancy is proportional to heat minus load, so altitude is managed entirely by anticipation. Horizontal motion comes from the atmosphere: define 4 stacked wind bands with different speeds and directions (some blowing left, some right), visualized by faint drifting streak particles in each band and a wind-stack indicator in the HUD, so navigating means climbing or sinking into the band that blows the way you want. Add 3-4 thermal columns rising above sun-warmed fields, rendered as subtle shimmering updraft particles, which give strong lift when entered — and cold sink over the lake. The course: pass through 6 numbered ring gates placed at varying altitudes across the landscape, then touch down gently on the finish pad (landing harder than a speed threshold bounces and scuffs one of 3 hull points; hitting mountainsides or the tall pines also costs hull). Burner fuel is limited but replenished by each gate collected. HUD: heat gauge, fuel bar, altimeter, vertical-speed needle, gates collected out of 6, hull points, and elapsed time; finish shows a time-and-hull scorecard with a retry option, and losing all hull deflates the balloon into a game-over card. Synthesize WebAudio: a roaring filtered-noise burner while held, wind whistle whose gain follows airspeed, a gate chime, and a creaking basket on hard bumps. Use delta-time physics so drift and heating are framerate-independent, style it as a watercolor dawn with layered parallax hills, and include an always-visible Restart button.

title: Kaleidobrush
brief: Every stroke you draw blooms into perfect N-fold mandala symmetry in real time.
prompt: Build a complete symmetry drawing studio as a single self-contained HTML file (all CSS and JS inline, no external resources, runs fully offline). A large centered square canvas on a near-black backdrop mirrors every stroke live into radial symmetry: as the user draws with mouse or touch, replicate the stroke into N rotated copies around the canvas center (N selectable from 4, 6, 8, 12, 16 via labeled buttons) with an additional mirror-reflection toggle for true kaleidoscope symmetry, rendering all copies simultaneously during the stroke, not after. Provide 3 distinct brushes chosen from a toolbar: RIBBON (a smooth polyline whose width tapers with drawing speed), NEON (a bright core with an additive outer glow), and STARDUST (a spray of small fading particles along the path); plus a brush size slider and an eraser. Color modes: a fixed color picker, and a FLOW mode where hue cycles gradually along the stroke length so long strokes rainbow naturally; background toggle between near-black and warm paper white with strokes compositing appropriately. Show faint guide spokes marking the symmetry sectors, hideable with a toggle. Support at least 30 steps of undo and a full clear (with confirm), and keep drawing smooth by stamping interpolated points between pointer events so fast strokes never break up. HUD: current symmetry N, mirror on/off, brush name, and stroke count. Include a one-click PNG export of the artwork without the guides, and a surprise-me button that draws a short procedural seed stroke to demonstrate the current settings. Animate a subtle slow rotation toggle (the whole artwork gently spins for display, pausable) driven by delta-time so it is framerate-independent. Style the chrome as a minimal dark studio with the artwork as the hero, and ensure complete mouse/touch parity including multi-segment touch strokes.

title: Marble Music Machine
brief: Pin bouncy pegs on a wall, drop marbles, and sculpt a melody out of pure physics.
prompt: Build a complete physics music toy as a single self-contained HTML file (all CSS and JS inline, no external resources, runs fully offline). A tall canvas wall: an emitter at the top drops marbles at a steady adjustable interval (0.5 to 4 seconds, slider), and each marble falls under gravity with delta-time integration, bouncing off pegs the user places. Click or tap empty space to place a round peg; drag existing pegs to move them; press-and-hold (or right-click) deletes one; cap pegs at 40 and live marbles at 12 (oldest removed). Every marble-peg collision plays a synthesized WebAudio pluck: pitch is the peg's vertical position quantized to a pentatonic scale over two octaves (higher on screen = higher pitch), stereo pan follows the peg's horizontal position, and velocity of impact scales volume — so the wall of pegs IS the instrument and identical drops replay the same cascading melody, making compositions repeatable. On each hit the peg flashes, rings with a brief expanding circle, and the marble sheds a few spark particles. Provide scale buttons (major pentatonic, minor pentatonic, whole-tone) that retune all pegs, a root-note selector, marbles-per-drop control (1 or 2 offset emitters), and a gravity slider for slow dreamy or fast plinky feels. A floor gutter quietly removes marbles with a soft tick. HUD: peg count, live marble count, notes played, plus Mute. Include 2 preset arrangements loadable from buttons (a descending arpeggio wall and a bouncing zigzag) authored as inline data so the toy demos itself, a Clear Pegs button, and a Reset that restores defaults. Style: dark slate wall, brass pegs with radial sheen, glass marbles with a moving highlight, soft bloom on flashes; identical behavior for mouse and touch.

title: Constellation Atlas
brief: Connect the stars of a procedural night sky into constellations you name and chart yourself.
prompt: Build a complete constellation-drawing studio as a single self-contained HTML file (all CSS and JS inline, no external resources, runs fully offline). Generate a seeded procedural night sky on a full-viewport canvas: about 400 stars with power-law brightness distribution, color temperature tinting (blue-white through amber), subtle individual twinkle, and a faint Milky-Way band of low-alpha haze; the same seed always yields the same sky, with the seed shown and a reseed button. Pan by dragging empty sky and zoom with wheel or pinch (0.5x to 3x). CONSTELLATION MODE (default): click or tap a star to start a constellation, then click further stars to chain glowing line segments between them (snapping to the nearest star within a small radius); clicking the previous star undoes a segment, double-click or a Finish button completes the figure, and a name input labels it in elegant small-caps beside its centroid. Completed constellations render their lines in a soft gold and their member stars slightly brighter; the studio supports at least 12 saved constellations listed in a side panel showing name, star count, and brightest-star designation, with hover/tap highlighting the figure on the sky, a rename field, and a delete control. Hovering any star shows a tooltip with its procedural designation (like HD-4471), magnitude, and color class. Include a CHART MODE toggle that re-renders everything as an antique paper star chart: parchment background, ink stars sized by magnitude, constellation lines in fine ink with serif labels and a decorative border — same data, new dress. Provide PNG export of either mode, a shooting star every 20-40 seconds for life, and gentle ambient sky-drift rotation (toggleable) driven by delta-time. HUD: star count under cursor region, constellation count, zoom level, and mode. Include a Clear All (confirmed) reset; full mouse and touch parity throughout.

title: Particle Foundry
brief: Type a word and watch four thousand particles storm into place to spell it — then smash it apart.
prompt: Build a complete particle typography toy as a single self-contained HTML file (all CSS and JS inline, no external resources, runs fully offline). A text input (up to 14 characters, default word FOUNDRY) drives the scene: rasterize the entered text once to an offscreen canvas in a heavy bold font, sample its filled pixels into about 4000 target points, and assign each target to a particle. Particles seek their targets with spring steering plus velocity damping so the word visibly assembles from chaos over about 2 seconds, each particle easing into place; re-entering new text smoothly re-assigns particles to the new letterforms (excess particles park offscreen, shortfall recruits them back). The cursor (or touch point) repels particles within a radius so the user can carve holes and smear the word, and particles flow back when released; a SCATTER button explodes everything outward with one impulse before re-forming, and holding S applies continuous turbulence. Provide 3 material presets as labeled buttons that change physics and rendering together: EMBERS (warm orange-to-white particles with additive glow, slight upward drift, and heat flicker), DEW (cool cyan droplets with gravity wobble and specular dots that sag slightly below their targets), and FIREFLIES (green-gold motes that wander gently around their targets with individual phase blinking). Sliders control particle count (1000-6000, rebuilding assignments live) and repel radius; a hue-shift slider tints any preset. HUD: live FPS, particle count, and active preset. Synthesize a soft WebAudio whoosh whose gain tracks average particle speed, behind a mute toggle. Use delta-time integration and a spatial-friendly loop over typed arrays so 4000+ particles stay smooth, handle window resize by re-rasterizing, style the chrome minimal and dark with the word as hero, and include a Reset button restoring the default word and settings with full mouse/touch parity.

title: Bonsai Studio
brief: Grow, prune, and wire a little procedural tree through the seasons into your own bonsai.
prompt: Build a complete bonsai cultivation studio as a single self-contained HTML file (all CSS and JS inline, no external resources, runs fully offline). A procedural tree grows slowly in a ceramic pot at the center of a calm canvas scene: model it as a branch node graph where each branch has length, angle, and thickness; every several seconds a bud at a branch tip either extends or forks with slight seeded randomness, thickness increases with the subtree it supports, and foliage pads (clusters of small leaf shapes) render at mature tips. The tree sways gently in a light breeze via per-branch phase offsets driven by delta-time. The player shapes it with 3 tools chosen from a toolbar: PRUNE (click or tap a branch to cut it — the severed subtree detaches and falls with simple tumbling physics before fading, and future growth redistributes to remaining tips), WIRE (drag from a branch to bend its angle smoothly within limits, shown with a coppery wire wrap that stays visible), and PINCH (click a foliage pad to compact it, densifying leaves). A seasons dial cycles SPRING (pale blossoms sprinkle the pads), SUMMER (deep green), AUTUMN (pads turn amber and occasionally shed a drifting leaf), and WINTER (bare structure with a snow dusting on top-facing branches) — season affects colors and shed particles only, growth continues. Include 3 pot styles and 2 backdrop moods (paper white, dusk gradient) as toggles. HUD: tree age in seasons, branch count, cuts made, and the growth seed; a NEW SEEDLING button (confirmed) restarts with a fresh seed, and PNG export captures the scene cleanly. Synthesize sparse WebAudio: a soft snip on prune, a creak on wire bends, and an ambient wind wash tied to sway amplitude, behind a mute toggle. Keep interaction identical for mouse and touch, ease all camera and growth motion with delta-time, and style the whole studio with quiet zen restraint.

title: Flipbook Machine
brief: Draw frame by frame with onion-skin ghosts, then hit play and watch your doodle come alive.
prompt: Build a complete frame-by-frame animation studio as a single self-contained HTML file (all CSS and JS inline, no external resources, runs fully offline). Center a fixed-size drawing canvas styled like a flipbook page. Drawing: a pen tool with 3 sizes and a 6-swatch color palette, an eraser, smooth interpolated strokes between pointer events (mouse and touch identical), and at least 20 steps of per-frame undo. Frames: start with 8 blank frames shown as a filmstrip of live thumbnails along the bottom (thumbnails update as the user draws); click a thumbnail to jump to that frame, with controls to add a frame after the current one, duplicate the current frame, delete it (confirmed, minimum 2 frames), and drag thumbnails to reorder; support up to 24 frames. Onion-skinning is the heart: overlay the previous frame in translucent red and the next frame in translucent blue beneath the active drawing layer, each toggleable, with an opacity slider — so in-betweening feels natural. Playback: a play/pause button loops the sequence at an adjustable frame rate (2 to 24 fps slider) with the current frame highlighted in the strip while playing; add a loop/ping-pong toggle and a large PRESENT mode that hides all chrome and plays the animation big until dismissed. Include a light-table background toggle (plain white or subtle dot grid that is never exported), a stamp of the frame number in a corner of the workspace (not the artwork), and PNG export of the current frame plus a one-click export of all frames tiled into a single sprite-sheet PNG with a labeled grid. Seed the app with a tiny 6-frame bouncing-ball example loadable from a button so playback demos instantly. HUD: current frame / total, fps, and tool. Provide a New Flipbook reset (confirmed). Keep the chrome friendly and paper-toned; drive playback timing with delta-time accumulation so the fps setting is honored regardless of display refresh.

title: Snow Globe Forge
brief: Assemble a tiny world inside the glass, then shake it and let the blizzard settle.
prompt: Build a complete snow-globe maker as a single self-contained HTML file (all CSS and JS inline, no external resources, runs fully offline). Render a large glass globe on a carved wooden base against a dusky room backdrop: draw the glass with a rim highlight, a soft top specular arc, and slight edge distortion shading so it reads as a sphere. FORGE MODE: a palette of procedurally drawn scene pieces — pine trees (3 sizes), a snowcapped cottage with warm lit windows, a rounded mountain, a snowman, a lamppost with a glow halo, and a crescent moon that pins to the sky — placed by selecting a chip then clicking/tapping inside the globe (pieces clamp to the globe's interior, ground pieces snap to the snowy floor arc, up to 12 pieces); drag placed pieces to adjust, press-and-hold or right-click to remove one. SNOW: about 800 flakes of varied size and depth (parallax layers) drift with gentle turbulence and settle onto the floor mound and briefly onto piece tops; a blizzard slider scales flake count and wind. SHAKE: grab and drag the globe itself — the whole scene lurches with damped spring wobble while pointer velocity injects swirl into the flakes, so a hard shake whites out the scene and slowly clears; a SHAKE button does a canned vigorous shake for touch users. Extras: day/night toggle retinting the whole scene, a music-box toggle playing a soft synthesized WebAudio celeste melody (8-note loop, slightly detuned, behind the toggle), and an engraving text field rendered in serif on a brass plate on the base. HUD: piece count and flake count. Include PNG export of the composed globe, a randomize-scene button, and a Clear Globe reset (confirmed). Drive wobble, swirl, and settling with delta-time so motion is framerate-independent, and keep mouse and touch interactions identical.

title: Planet Forge
brief: Sculpt a living world with sliders — oceans rise, ice spreads, rings tilt, moons swing by.
prompt: Build a complete planet designer as a single self-contained HTML file (all CSS and JS inline, no external resources, runs fully offline). Center a large rotating planet rendered on canvas: generate seeded multi-octave value noise as an equirectangular terrain map on an offscreen buffer, then each frame sample it orthographically onto the visible disc with longitude advancing over time so the planet turns (about one rotation per 40 seconds, delta-time driven), shaded by a light direction with a soft terminator into a night side. Control panel sliders regenerate or restyle the world live: SEA LEVEL (flooding lowlands into oceans with coastal shallows tinted lighter), ICE CAPS (polar latitude threshold with crackled edges), ATMOSPHERE (rim-glow thickness and haze strength), CLOUD COVER (a second independently drifting noise layer of soft white clouds), and TERRAIN HUE (shifting the land palette from lush greens through ochre desert to alien violet). Toggles add a RING SYSTEM (a banded ellipse with correct occlusion — hidden behind the planet's upper half, passing in front below — plus a tilt slider) and 0-3 MOONS that orbit with distinct radii, sizes, and periods, disappearing behind the planet believably. A NIGHT LIGHTS toggle sprinkles warm city-light speckles on dark-side landmasses above sea level. Show a name input and a generated stats card (radius, gravity, day length, mean temperature — plausible flavor values derived deterministically from the seed and sliders). Buttons: REROLL SEED (new terrain, sliders kept), RANDOMIZE ALL, PNG export of the current view including rings and moons, and Reset to defaults. Keep it smooth: re-render the terrain buffer only when a terrain-affecting slider changes, and keep per-frame work to sampling and compositing. HUD: seed, FPS, and rotation toggle. Style the chrome as a dark mission-console with the planet as hero; sliders and toggles must work with mouse and touch identically.

title: Firefly Synchrony
brief: Three hundred fireflies blink out of phase — then, slowly, the whole meadow starts pulsing as one.
prompt: Build a complete emergent-synchronization animation as a single self-contained HTML file (all CSS and JS inline, no external resources, runs fully offline). A night meadow on a full-window canvas: a silhouetted grass fringe swaying gently at the bottom, a few dark tree shapes, faint stars. Scatter about 300 fireflies, each an integrate-and-fire oscillator: its internal phase climbs steadily (with slight per-firefly rate variation), and on reaching threshold it FLASHES — a warm gold bloom that swells and fades over about 300 milliseconds — and resets. Coupling creates the magic: when a firefly flashes, every neighbor within a radius nudges its own phase forward proportionally to a coupling strength, so from random beginnings the meadow drifts over a minute or two from chaotic twinkling into mesmerizing waves and finally near-unison pulsing. Make the emergence legible: a SYNC meter in the HUD computes and displays the live order parameter (0 percent = chaos, 100 percent = unison) as a slim gauge, and a scrolling 60-second sparkline records its history so the climb to synchrony is visible. Interaction: click or tap to scatter phases randomly within a burst radius (the meadow audibly and visibly falls back toward chaos there, then re-entrains); drag to gently herd fireflies, which drift with slow wander steering at all times; a coupling slider from zero (never syncs) to strong (locks fast) invites experimentation, and a density slider adjusts the population 100-500. Fireflies leave the faintest motion trails, and each flash lights the grass beneath it subtly. Synthesize WebAudio: a soft wooden tick per flash, rate-limited and mixed quieter as flash density rises, so full synchrony sounds like a single gentle heartbeat; mute toggle included. Use delta-time for phases, wander, and blooms so behavior is framerate-independent, handle resize, and include a Reset button that re-scatters positions and phases.

title: Aurora Veil
brief: Curtains of northern light ripple over a snowy ridge — drag the sky to send a solar gust through them.
prompt: Build a complete aurora borealis animation as a single self-contained HTML file (all CSS and JS inline, no external resources, runs fully offline). Fill the window with a polar night: a deep blue-black gradient sky over a snowy ridge silhouette, sparse stars with slow twinkle, and 2-4 aurora curtains as the centerpiece. Build each curtain from 100-200 vertical rays hung along a slowly undulating baseline path across the sky; each ray's height, brightness, and sway are driven by layered value noise you implement yourself (no libraries) sampled in ray-position and time, so the curtains continuously furl, split, and re-form organically. Color rays with a vertical gradient from luminous green at the base through teal to violet-pink tips, rendered with additive blending and a soft vertical falloff so overlapping curtains bloom brighter; reflect a faint blurred copy onto the snow below. Interaction: dragging horizontally across the sky (mouse or touch) launches a SOLAR GUST — a traveling disturbance that sweeps along the curtains in the drag direction, locally surging brightness, height, and sway before relaxing over a few seconds; clicking/tapping sparks a brief localized brightening; and a palette key or on-screen button cycles three schemes (classic green, rare all-red storm, blue-violet), crossfading over 2 seconds. Add a shooting star every 20-40 seconds and an occasional slow satellite dot. Keep it performant: render rays as canvas line strips at device-appropriate counts, target smooth animation via requestAnimationFrame, and drive every motion with delta-time so speed is framerate-independent. HUD: a minimal corner readout with palette name and an activity meter showing current aurora energy, plus a mute toggle for a synthesized WebAudio wind-and-shimmer bed (filtered noise whose brightness follows aurora energy). Handle resize gracefully and include a Reset button restoring the default palette and calm conditions.

title: The Four-Season Tree
brief: One tree lives a whole year in sixty seconds — buds, shade, fire, snow, and back again.
prompt: Build a complete perpetual-seasons animation as a single self-contained HTML file (all CSS and JS inline, no external resources, runs fully offline). Grow one procedurally generated deciduous tree (recursive branching with seeded jitter, tapering limb thickness, gentle per-branch wind sway) on a low hill, and cycle it through a full year every 60 seconds, seamlessly looping. SPRING: pale green buds pop along branches in a staggered cascade, unfurling into small leaves while white petals occasionally drift from a brief blossom phase; the sky warms and birdsong-like synthesized chirps play sparsely. SUMMER: the canopy thickens to deep green with dappled highlight speckles that shimmer, cumulus clouds drift, and heat shimmer subtly wobbles the horizon. AUTUMN: leaves turn individually through gold to crimson on their own schedules, then release — each falling leaf tumbles with its own rotation, drag, and flutter physics, landing to build a persistent leaf litter that drifts in gusts. WINTER: bare branches accumulate snow along top-facing edges, snowfall of parallax-layered flakes settles on ground and litter, breath-of-wind gusts shake loose snow puffs, and nights lengthen with a cooler palette; then thaw drips lead back to spring. Tie sky gradient, sun height, cloud style, and ground palette to a continuous season clock driven by delta-time so every transition crossfades smoothly with no popping. Controls: a season wheel HUD showing the current position in the year with a draggable handle to scrub time (mouse and touch), 1x/4x speed buttons and pause, a wind slider that visibly affects sway, leaf fall, and snowflakes, and a NEW TREE button that regenerates with a fresh seed while keeping the clock. Add a small readout of the current season and day. Synthesize all ambience with WebAudio behind a mute toggle: sparse spring chirps, summer insect drone, autumn wind rustle whose gain tracks falling-leaf count, and winter hush with a low wind moan.

title: Paper Crane
brief: A square of paper folds itself, crease by crease, into a crane — then flaps away.
prompt: Build a complete self-folding origami animation as a single self-contained HTML file (all CSS and JS inline, no external resources, runs fully offline). On a serene desk-scene canvas (soft wood tones, a pool of warm lamp light), a square sheet of paper folds itself into a stylized crane through a sequence of at least 8 clearly readable steps (diagonal fold, second diagonal, petal-style narrowing, wing folds, head crease and so on — a stylized 2.5D interpretation, not rigorous origami math). Represent the sheet as a small set of polygon faces; animate each step by rotating the moving face group about its crease line with smooth easing, scaling the faces' rendered shape to suggest lift off the table, and shading each face by its simulated facing (front side warm white, reverse a chosen accent color, with a soft moving shadow under lifted parts) so every fold reads as paper turning in space. Between steps, flash the upcoming crease as a dashed line and show a caption naming the step in a small card (Step 3 of 8 — Fold wings down). When the crane completes, it rests a beat, then its wings flap with an eased hinge motion, it lifts off, and it flies away along a gentle arc with a drifting paper-flutter before a fresh sheet of a NEW accent color floats down onto the desk and the cycle repeats endlessly. Controls: click or tap advances to the next step immediately (or pauses mid-flight), a play/pause button, a scrub slider that moves continuously through the entire fold sequence in both directions (mouse and touch), a speed control, and 4 paper choices (plain, polka dot, indigo wave pattern, sunset gradient — all procedurally drawn). Synthesize WebAudio behind a mute toggle: a crisp paper-crease swish per fold, a soft flap whoosh, and a tiny chime at completion. Drive all easing with delta-time so the piece is framerate-independent, handle resize, and include a Restart button that returns to the flat sheet.

title: Coral Bloom
brief: Watch branching coral accrete particle by particle on the seafloor, under drifting caustic light.
prompt: Build a complete diffusion-limited-aggregation coral garden as a single self-contained HTML file (all CSS and JS inline, no external resources, runs fully offline). On a full-window undersea canvas (deep teal gradient darkening with depth, a sandy floor band, drifting caustic light ripples overlaid as slowly morphing bright webs near the top), grow coral by DLA: seed 3 coral anchors on the seafloor, then continuously release invisible walker particles that random-walk through the water until they touch existing coral, where they stick and become a permanent coral cell — so branching, fan-like structures accrete visibly over time, each anchor growing its own colony. Use an occupancy grid for O(1) stickiness checks and bias walker spawn positions near active growth fronts so structure emerges steadily (target roughly 60-150 new cells per second, with total cells capped around 20000). Color each colony from a distinct palette (magenta-to-pale-pink, amber-to-cream, violet-to-lavender) with cells tinted by their age — old cores deep and saturated, fresh tips nearly white — so growth history is written in the gradient; give tips a soft glow pulse. A CURRENT slider tilts the random walk directionally, visibly bending new growth downstream like real current-swept coral, and a click or tap plants a new coral seed at the pointer (up to 6 colonies, each drawing the next palette). Add life: 6-10 small fish silhouettes cruising in gentle sine paths with occasional direction flips, a stream of tiny rising bubbles, and drifting marine-snow motes for parallax. HUD: total coral cells, colony count, growth rate per second, and elapsed time; include Pause, a 4x time-lapse toggle, and a Reset button that clears to bare sand and reseeds. Synthesize a WebAudio underwater ambience (low filtered noise with slow swells plus sparse soft clicks as colonies hit growth milestones) behind a mute toggle. Advance walkers with a fixed-step accumulator fed by delta-time so growth speed is framerate-independent, and handle window resize.

title: Pendulum Wave
brief: Fifteen pendulums drift out of step into snakes and weaves, then swing back into one perfect line.
prompt: Build a complete pendulum-wave physics animation as a single self-contained HTML file (all CSS and JS inline, no external resources, runs fully offline). Hang 15 pendulums side by side from a polished rail across the top of the canvas, with lengths precisely tuned so that over one full cycle (default 60 seconds) pendulum k completes exactly N plus k oscillations (N defaulting to 51) — released together from the same amplitude, they drift through traveling waves, double helixes, ordered chaos, and back into a single unified line at the cycle's end. Compute each pendulum analytically from elapsed time (angle = amplitude times cosine of its angular frequency times t) so the pattern is exact, deterministic, and never accumulates drift; advance time with delta-time. Render side-on: fine wires from the rail to glossy bobs colored in a rainbow sweep across the row, each bob with a specular dot and a soft floor shadow that stretches with swing; behind them draw a faint dark backdrop with a center line so the alignment moments read clearly. Add a TRACE toggle that draws fading light streaks along each bob's recent path, turning the pattern into flowing ribbon art, and a TOP VIEW toggle that switches to a plan view where the bobs form a serpentine phase snake. Show a cycle progress ring counting down to the grand re-alignment, and fire a subtle full-row shimmer plus a soft synthesized WebAudio chime exactly at re-unison (mute toggle available). Controls: cycle length slider (30-120 seconds, retuning lengths live), pendulum count slider (9-21), amplitude slider, pause, and an instant RELEASE button that restarts all pendulums together from rest at full amplitude. HUD: cycle time, count, and a phase readout naming the current regime (unison, traveling wave, alternating, chaos) by simple phase heuristics. Handle resize, keep every control usable by mouse and touch, and style it as a museum brass-and-walnut demonstration piece.

title: Murmuration
brief: Two thousand starlings breathe as one shape-shifting cloud over a dusk marsh — until the falcon dives.
prompt: Build a complete starling-murmuration animation as a single self-contained HTML file (all CSS and JS inline, no external resources, runs fully offline). Simulate at least 2000 boids over a dusk marsh scene: a warm orange-to-violet gradient sky, a low sun disc, a dark reed-bed silhouette along the bottom, and a bare tree at one side. Implement alignment, cohesion, and separation using a uniform spatial grid for neighbor lookups and typed arrays for state so the flock stays smooth at count; add a soft home attraction keeping the flock on screen, mild wind drift, and per-bird speed limits with banking-style turn smoothing. Render each starling as a tiny elongated dark fleck oriented to its velocity, and modulate local rendering density into the murmuration look: where birds crowd, draw them darker and slightly larger so the flock reads as a shape-shifting charcoal cloud with translucent thin edges, exactly like real murmurations pulsing between dense ribbons and open veils. THE FALCON: an autonomous predator with its own hunting logic circles high, then periodically stoops through the densest region; birds within its fear radius flee hard, tearing dramatic voids and split-and-rejoin waves through the flock — the signature spectacle. A toggle hands the falcon to the pointer so the user can dive it by hand (mouse or touch); otherwise it hunts on its own timer. Controls: sliders for cohesion strength, fear radius, and bird count (500-3000, live), plus pause and a Reset button that re-scatters the flock. HUD: bird count, live FPS, and a flock-density meter; keep everything framerate-independent with delta-time steering. Synthesize a WebAudio bed behind a mute toggle: layered wing-flutter noise whose intensity follows average flock speed, and a thin falcon cry when a stoop begins. Handle resize; the whole piece should feel like slow, hypnotic breathing punctuated by moments of panic.

title: Gearworks
brief: A brass gear train assembles tooth by tooth, meshing perfectly — crank the flywheel and wake the whole machine.
prompt: Build a complete self-assembling clockwork animation as a single self-contained HTML file (all CSS and JS inline, no external resources, runs fully offline). On a dark walnut panel canvas, a brass gear train constructs itself: gears fly in one at a time with eased arcs and settle so their teeth mesh exactly with an existing gear, growing a connected train of about 10-14 gears of varied radii (draw each gear procedurally with a proper tooth profile — trapezoidal teeth are fine — plus a hub, 3-5 spokes, and rim rivets; tooth size must be IDENTICAL across all gears so meshing is geometrically honest, with tooth counts proportional to radius). Drive rotation kinematically from a single source: a large flywheel turns at the base rate, and every meshed neighbor rotates at the correct ratio (angular velocity inversely proportional to tooth count) in the opposite direction, with tooth phases aligned at contact points so meshes never visibly clip — the correctness of the motion is the beauty. Along the way the train powers charming endpoints: one branch turns a clock hand on a small dial, another bobs a piston, and the final gear rings a tiny bell once per revolution. Interaction: drag the flywheel to crank speed up or down with momentum (mouse or touch), a reverse button flips direction with an eased slowdown-and-reverse, clicking near the rim of any placed gear grows one new correctly sized meshing gear at that contact angle if space allows (up to a cap of 18), and a rebuild button clears and reassembles a new random train from a fresh seed. HUD: gear count, current flywheel RPM, and total revolutions. Light everything with a soft top-left sheen, moving glints along rims, and drop shadows between overlapping layers. Synthesize WebAudio behind a mute toggle: a fine ticking whose tempo tracks flywheel speed, a satisfying chunk when a new gear seats, and the little bell. Advance all rotation with delta-time so ratios stay exact at any framerate, handle resize, and include a Reset button.
